data.title: Costco CFO says consumers remain steady as value focus, digital growth, global expansion support momentum
data.description:
NEW YORK – Costco (COST) sees a consumer who remains willing to spend when the value is clear, even as shoppers navigate uncertainty around tariffs, calendars, gas prices and the broader economy, Chief Financial Officer Gary Millerchip said in a livestreamed interview Thursday at the UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference.

Speaking to UBS analyst Michael Lasser, Millerchip said Costco’s (COST) business has held up with notable consistency over the past year, despite the shifting headlines that have shaped investor and consumer sentiment.

“When you strip out the different noise elements,” he said, members have been “remarkably consistent,” continuing to spend on both staples and discretionary items when Costco (COST) delivers strong value, quality and fresh merchandise.

That resilience has helped reinforce Costco’s (COST) standing as one of the most closely watched names in retail, especially as other companies offer more mixed assessments of consumer health.

Millerchip said Costco’s (COST) members still appear to have spending capacity, but that the burden remains on the retailer’s merchants and operators to keep earning those dollars. In nonfoods especially, he said, shoppers keep responding when they find compelling prices and a sense of discovery.

Costco's (COST) stock this year is up 16%, contrasting with a 2.5% decline for the Standard & Poor's 500 stock index (SP500).

Gas business remains a loyalty and traffic engine

Costco’s (COST) fuel business continues to work in both high and low price environments, though in different ways, Millerchip said.

When gas prices rise, he said, Costco (COST) benefits from a stronger perception of price advantage, which can encourage members to drive farther to fill up. When prices fall, members may have more discretionary income to spend inside the warehouse, even if some are less motivated to make a special trip for fuel alone.

About half of Costco (COST) gas transactions result in a warehouse visit during the same trip, Millerchip said, underscoring the strategic role gas plays in driving both traffic and loyalty.

He added that Costco (COST) is working to improve congestion and throughput at both warehouses and fuel stations through technology and expansion projects. Among those efforts are checkout improvements, gas station expansions, new pump technology and geofencing initiatives intended to speed visits.

Long runway for growth

Asked about the durability of Costco’s (COST) recent success, Millerchip pointed to several avenues for continued growth, including merchandising, digital capabilities and international store openings.

He said Costco (COST) is still finding ways to add value to membership, citing benefits such as extended shopping hours for executive members and Instacart credits. The goal, he said, is to make joining or renewing a Costco (COST) membership an easy financial decision.

Millerchip also said e-commerce remains a meaningful growth opportunity. While digital sales still make up a relatively small portion of Costco’s (COST) total revenue, he said the company sees room to improve assortment, technology and the online member experience.

He highlighted big and bulky delivery as one area where Costco can differentiate itself, noting that services such as delivery, installation and haul-away can add to the value proposition for members buying large items.

On physical expansion, Millerchip said Costco (COST) still sees a path to opening at least about 30 warehouses a year over the next 5 to 10 years, with opportunities across the U.S. and in international markets including Europe, Asia, Australia, Mexico and Canada.

He said Costco (COST) isn’t focused on entering a new country immediately, but left open the possibility of future geographic expansion beyond its current footprint.

Traffic dips are watched, but not overread

Millerchip said visits remain an important measure of business health, but cautioned against drawing broad conclusions from any single month.

He noted that weather disruptions, especially in the Northeast, affected some recent traffic patterns, including temporary warehouse closures. Even so, he said Costco (COST) did not see anything in the latest month that called for a sharp reaction.

Rather than responding to short-term swings, Millerchip said Costco (COST) remains focused on continuously improving value and reducing friction in the shopping experience.

That includes efforts to increase warehouse efficiency, improve checkout speed, expand capacity in busy locations and keep basket sizes healthy. He said the company is seeing larger baskets driven more by item growth and mix than by inflation, which supports Costco’s efficiency-driven business model.

Digital strategy broadens, but stays true to the brand

Millerchip acknowledged that Costco (COST) has become more aggressive in digital commerce after historically taking a cautious approach.

He said part of that shift reflects work the company has done to modernize its technology foundation. But he stressed that Costco (COST) still views the warehouse as central to the brand and believes physical retail works when merchants give customers a reason to visit.

For Costco (COST), e-commerce is meant to complement the warehouse rather than replace it, he said. That means a curated online assortment, not an attempt to be everything to everyone.

Costco’s (COST) digital business is growing faster than the rest of the company, Millerchip said, and management believes it is largely incremental rather than cannibalistic. He said even services such as same-day grocery delivery through Instacart generally add trips instead of merely shifting them online.

The company has also become more comfortable with personalization, he said, as members increasingly expect more relevant communications and digital experiences.

AI seen as opportunity, not threat

Millerchip said Costco(COST) views artificial intelligence, including large language models and agentic shopping, more as an opportunity than a risk.

Because Costco (COST) doesn’t rely on paid search as a marketing model, he said the company is focused on making sure its value proposition is accurately represented in AI-driven search and recommendation tools.

That means working with major AI platforms to improve data quality, product descriptions and visibility so that Costco (COST) shows up appropriately when consumers search for items such as washers, dryers or other high-ticket products.

Millerchip said Costco (COST) believes AI could help the company surface more often in product discovery because of its pricing, reviews and service advantages, such as free delivery, installation and haul-away on certain items.

Inside the business, he said Costco (COST) is also using AI to improve productivity, especially in areas like call center operations. But he suggested that most of the economic benefit from those efficiencies would likely be reinvested into the business and member experience rather than allowed to simply expand margins.

“We’re primarily a top-line company,” Millerchip said, describing reinvestment as a core part of Costco’s (COST) flywheel.

Digital signups reshape membership mix

Millerchip also addressed a topic closely watched by investors: membership renewal trends.

He said Costco (COST) hasn’t seen a meaningful effect from its recent membership fee increase. Instead, changes in renewal rates have been driven more by a changing mix of members, especially a larger share of digitally acquired shoppers.

Where digital signups once represented less than 5% of new memberships, they now account for more than 40%, Millerchip said. He added that nearly half of new members are under 40, helping lower the average age of Costco’s (COST) membership base and supporting faster overall member growth.

Those digitally acquired members tend to renew at lower rates initially because they are less engaged than members who sign up in warehouses and shop more frequently, he said. But Costco (COST) has been working to improve those outcomes through better onboarding, communication and renewal efforts.

Millerchip said recent trends suggest some stabilization, and he expressed confidence that the company can keep improving digital member engagement over time.

Conviction remains high

Nearly two years into his tenure as CFO, Millerchip said his confidence in Costco’s (COST) long-term model has only strengthened.

He said the company’s founding principles remain as relevant today as they were decades ago: deliver strong value to members, take care of employees and produce good outcomes for shareholders as a result.

At the same time, he said Costco (COST) has shown it can evolve, whether through gas, fresh food, private label, digital investments or new technologies.

For Millerchip, that balance between consistency and adaptation is what supports Costco’s (COST) staying power.

“The company has stayed true to those principles, but it’s evolved over time,” he said. “The principles that create value are absolutely as true today.”

data.title: American Public Education outlines $685M–$695M 2026 revenue guidance as new campus expansion and segment realignment advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Public Education, Inc. (APEI) (APEI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Angela Selden, President and CEO, emphasized that "APUS delivered full year 2025 revenue growth, even with the fourth quarter registration interruption." Selden highlighted that Rasmussen's full year 2025 revenue grew 14% and Hondros' increased 11%, contributing to consolidated revenue growth of 4% to $649 million, despite the midyear sale of Graduate School USA and the announced closure of two Rasmussen campuses. Selden noted, "APEI's full year adjusted EBITDA reached $85.7 million, up 19% as compared to 2024, beating not only our revised guidance, but also beating the top end of our initial 2025 guidance." The CEO underscored fulfillment of commitments to redeem preferred equity, sell corporate buildings, and secure the Department of Education's lifting of a $25 million letter of credit and growth restrictions, stating, "Achievement of these commitments has simplified and strengthened our business for 2026 and beyond."
  • Selden announced the launch of two new campuses in 2026: a Rasmussen campus in Orlando and a Hondros campus in Detroit, with future plans to enroll students as early as 2Q '26 and Q1 '27, respectively. She also reported a segment realignment: "We are formally announcing that we have 2 reporting segments, APU Global and RU Health+ for fiscal year 2026 and beyond."
  • CFO Edward Codispoti stated, "Total revenue in the fourth quarter was $158.3 million, down $5.8 million or 3.5% compared to $164.1 million in the prior year period. Despite the federal government shutdown impact at APUS, we exceeded our most recently stated guidance." Codispoti also highlighted, "Fourth quarter net income available to common stockholders was $12.6 million or $0.67 per diluted share compared to $11.5 million or $0.63 per diluted share in the prior year period." He further noted a refinancing of debt reducing the borrowing rate by approximately 375 basis points, lowering principal from $96.4 million to $90 million, and introducing a $50 million share repurchase program.

Outlook

  • The company provided full year 2026 guidance: "APEI 2026 revenue will be between $685 million and $695 million. Adjusted EBITDA will be between $91.5 million and $100.5 million," according to Selden. Q1 2026 guidance was set at revenue of $173 million to $175 million and adjusted EBITDA of $25.5 million to $27 million. Codispoti added, "Net income available to common stockholders between $41.3 million and $47.6 million, adjusted EBITDA between $91.5 million and $100.5 million, diluted earnings per share between $2.15 per share and $2.47 per share and CapEx between $28 million and $32 million."
  • Management expects continued enrollment momentum and margin expansion, especially supported by new campus openings and integration of Rasmussen and Hondros.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter consolidated revenue was $158.3 million. At APUS, revenue was $71 million, down 13.8%, attributed to the federal government shutdown. Rasmussen's revenue was $66.6 million, up 15.9%, driven by 8.9% enrollment growth to 15,900 students. Hondros reported $20.7 million in revenue, up 9.2% with 4,000 students.
  • Fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA was $28.7 million, with a margin of 18.1%. Full year 2025 consolidated revenue was $648.9 million, up 3.9%. Adjusted EBITDA reached $85.7 million, a margin expansion to 13.2%. Net income for the full year was $25.3 million or $1.36 per diluted share.
  • The balance sheet ended 2025 with $176.5 million in cash and equivalents, and total debt of $96.4 million. The company expects annual interest expense savings of $3.7 million from the refinancing.

Q&A

  • Griffin Boss, B. Riley Securities: Asked about CapEx cadence for new campus openings. Gary Janson, SVP of Growth, replied, "Most of the CapEx related to the new campuses would be in the fourth quarter, maybe a little bit in the third quarter."
  • Boss followed up on campus economics. Janson explained, "We expect them to be -- cost about $3.5 million to open, take about 18 months before we turn cash flow positive... at scale, we would expect the campus to do about $12 million in revenue and have about a 35% EBITDA margin."
  • Lucas John Horton, Northland Capital: Queried on marketing strategy post-institution combination. Selden responded that Rasmussen and Hondros brands will remain, and best practices from each unit will be shared to optimize spend.
  • Eric Martinuzzi, Lake Street Capital: Asked about non-TA side enrollments. Janson confirmed about 11% growth, with high-teen registration growth in veterans and extended family markets.
  • Rajiv Sharma, Texas Capital: Inquired about the "Fill the Back Row" strategy. Selden outlined continued marketing investments targeting high demand programs, while Janson added that cross-pollination of programs and expansion of offerings will drive growth.
  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: Sought quantification of the government shutdown revenue impact. Codispoti stated, "We ended up probably $12 million to $15 million short from the government impact."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, frequently congratulating management on strong results, but also pressed for details on CapEx, enrollment trends, marketing strategies, and potential impacts from government shutdowns. Questions focused on sustainability of growth and drivers behind guidance figures.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing operational execution and visibility into future growth. In Q&A, the tone was consistent, with clear explanations and readiness to provide specifics. Selden used phrases like, "The foundation is built, our business is simplified, and we are operating with a strong balance sheet."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management exhibited increased optimism, reflecting improved financial flexibility, guidance above previous targets, and progress on strategic initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter guidance is significantly higher than the revised guidance from the previous quarter. Management now projects 2026 revenue of $685 million to $695 million, compared to a prior full-year 2025 range of $640 million to $644 million given during the Q3 call.
  • There is a shift to a two-segment reporting structure from three segments previously. The new approach, APU Global and RU Health+, reflects ongoing integration efforts.
  • Management's tone has grown more confident, with stronger emphasis on execution and growth drivers, while analysts have shifted from immediate operational concerns (e.g., government shutdown, registration impacts) to longer-term growth and margin sustainability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the risk of further government funding disruptions, though recent experience suggests student demand rebounds swiftly after interruptions.
  • There is ongoing exposure to policy shifts and appropriations affecting Tuition Assistance for military students. Management described mitigation through diversified enrollment channels and strong demand from veterans and families.
  • Analysts noted potential margin pressure from ramp-up costs for new campus openings and investments in marketing.

Final Takeaway

American Public Education, Inc. closed 2025 with solid revenue and margin growth, resilient enrollment across its major institutions, and a strengthened balance sheet. Management delivered on key commitments, including cost reductions and strategic simplification, while setting out ambitious targets for 2026. With new campus openings planned and a streamlined reporting structure, the company is focused on execution and leveraging its diversified educational platforms for continued growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Emerald Expositions GAAP EPS of -$0.15 misses by $0.19, revenue of $132.7M beats by $1.7M
data.description:
• Emerald Expositions press release (EEX): Q4 GAAP EPS of -$0.15 misses by $0.19.
  • Revenue of $132.7M (+24.3% Y/Y) beats by $1.7M.
  • Revenues of $463.4 million, an increase of $64.6 million, or 16.2%, over the prior year period, primarily due to revenues from acquisitions and higher Organic Revenues.

data.title: Klarna chair Michael Moritz buys about $50M of shares
data.description:
• Klarna Group’s (KLAR) chairman Michael Moritz bought 3.47M ordinary shares worth about $50M through an associated entity between March 3 and March 11.
  • David Fock, the firm’s chief product & design officer, purchased 27,000 ordinary shares on March 9, 2026, for an aggregate consideration of $388,552.14.
  • Meanwhile, chief marketing officer David Sandström sold 32,703 shares on March 9, 2026, and chief commercial officer David Sykes sold 23,799 shares on March 13, 2026. Both sales were made pursuant to Rule 10b5-1 trading plans, established in 2025.
  • KLAR +7% premarket to $15.6.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: AM Markets Need to Know: Russian oil waivers, mineral trade deal, and more
data.description:
Stock index futures were mixed on Friday as investors prepared for a busy morning of economic data, with GDP and PCE figures topping the calendar.

Now, here are five news stories that broke overnight to watch out for:

Forced labor trade probe: The U.S. has launched trade investigations into 60 economies—including China, the European Union, Canada, Mexico, Japan, India and Russia—to determine whether they failed to ban imports of goods produced with forced labor. The investigations will be conducted under Section 301((b)) of the Trade Act of 1974, which enables the U.S. to impose tariffs on countries engaging in unfair practices that impact U.S. commerce.

Russian oil waivers: The United States has issued a 30-day waiver allowing countries to purchase sanctioned Russian oil and petroleum products already stranded at sea, expanding a temporary exemption granted last week to India alone. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a post on X that this was a “narrowly tailored, short-term measure” that applies only to oil already in transit.

ByteDance AI expansion: TikTok’s Chinese parent ByteDance (BDNCE) is assembling computing power with top Nvidia (NVDA) chips outside China, the Wall Street Journal reported on Thursday. ByteDance is working with Southeast Asian firm Aolani Cloud to deploy about 500 Nvidia Blackwell computing systems in Malaysia, totaling roughly 36,000 B200 chips.

Mineral trade agreement: The U.S., Japan, and the European Union are set to announce plans in the coming weeks to lay the foundation for a trade agreement in critical minerals, Bloomberg News reported. The Office of the U.S. Trade Representative, which has led negotiations with Brussels and Tokyo on the framework, will also head talks for a trade deal that is set to include a price floor and tariffs for the materials to counter any market distortions by China.

Amazon Prime Day timing: Amazon (AMZN) plans to shift its annual Prime Day sale to late June from July, sources told Bloomberg. A timing change would have broad implications across the retail sector as thousands of third-party vendors and competitors such as Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), Costco (COST) and Best Buy (BBY) consider whether they need to change their summer schedules.

data.title: PAR Technology stock dips after pricing $250M convertible debt offering
data.description:
PAR Technology (PAR) has priced a private offering of $250M aggregate principal amount of 4.00% Convertible Senior Notes due 2031.

The company also granted to the initial purchasers of the notes a 13-day option to purchase up to an additional $15M aggregate principal amount of notes. The sale is expected to close on March 17, 2026, subject to satisfaction of the conditions to closing.

The notes will mature on March 15, 2031, unless earlier converted, redeemed, or repurchased. Interest will accrue on the notes at 4.00% per year.

The initial conversion rate will be 52.5762 shares of the common stock per $1,000 principal amount of notes (equivalent to an initial conversion price of $19.02 per share of the common stock, which represents a conversion premium of 20.0% above the last reported sale price of the common stock on the New York Stock Exchange on March 12, 2026).

The company expects to receive about $242.3M in net proceeds from the offering (or about $256.8M if the option to purchase additional notes is fully exercised), after deducting discounts, commissions, and offering expenses.

It plans to use the proceeds to repurchase about $207.5M of its 1.50% Convertible Senior Notes due 2027, buy back roughly 2.09M shares of common stock for about $33.1M, and allocate the remaining funds for general corporate purposes.

PAR shares fell -20% premarket to $12.64 on Friday.

data.title: PagerDuty slips on soft revenue outlook, churn concerns despite Q4 beat
data.description:
Shares of PagerDuty (PD) slipped 13.5% in Friday's premarket trading despite reporting a quarterly beat, as the company issued a weaker-than-expected revenue forecast. Analysts also pointed to a high churn rate and softness in paid user licenses.

The company forecasts first-quarter revenue in the range of $118 million to $120 million, compared to the average analyst estimate of $123.5 million. Adjusted EPS is expected to be between $0.23 and $0.25 vs. estimate of $0.28.

Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of retention improvements, implications of pricing transitions, and the potential for flat revenue growth, with a tone best characterized as neutral to slightly cautious.

Morgan Stanley analyst Sanjit Singh rates the stock an equal weight and cut the PT to $7 from $9, arguing that growth is pressured by compression in paid user licenses, weaker gross retention, and mid-market churn, Bloomberg reported. PagerDuty (PD) also reported 4Q annual recurring revenue that missed estimates.

Meanwhile, RBC Capital Markets analyst Matthew Hedberg gives the stock a "sector perform" rating, trimming PT to $8 from $9. While PagerDuty reported a better-than-feared quarter with AI native/enterprise customer acceleration, there were continued headwinds related to paid user licenses, the brokerage said, according to media reports. RBC remains “cautious around macro pressures as the stock remains a show-me story.”

The stock is down 45% year to date and carries a Strong Sell rating from the SA Quant system, while Wall Street analysts rate it a Buy.

data.title: América Móvil unit eyes Azteca, Total Play fiber assets
data.description:
Mexico's América Móvil (AMX) considers buying telecom assets from Columbia-based Grupo Salinas, the conglomerate founded by Ricardo Salinas Pliego.

Comcel, a subsidiary of América Móvil (AMX) that operates under the Claro brand in Colombia, has requested authorization from the regulatory body to integrate strategic assets owned by Azteca Comunicaciones Colombia and Total Play Comunicaciones Colombia, a local newspaper reported.

The operation includes the acquisition of the so-called National Fiber Optic Project Network and an approximate extension of 20,000 kilometers, as well as the ACC Network of about 12,000 kilometers, both operated by Azteca Comunicaciones, a company of the Salinas Group.

The transaction also includes the transfer of contracts for the use and management of the fiber optic network, the contractual position in a commercial trust for administration and payments, as well as assets such as infrastructure, towers, and lease agreements. No valuation details are disclosed yet.

Salinas Pliego, the owner of Grupo Salinas, is monetizing assets following a $1.9B tax settlement with Mexico's government. If completed, the acquisition would deepen América Móvil’s footprint in Colombia.

data.title: New York City weighs raising minimum wage to $30 by 2030
data.description:
Remember the "Fight for $15" that started in New York City last decade? Well, the movement has evolved and the goalposts have moved, with "$30 by '30" being demanded in the Big Apple. Mayor Zohran Mamdani, a democratic socialist, already advocated for the new minimum wage level on the campaign trail, but the pledge is now being taken up at the New York City Council.

Rallying cry: If approved, the newly introduced bill would stagger wage increases over the next several years, bringing the minimum wage to "$30 an hour by 2030" - for businesses with over 500 workers. Smaller employers would have two additional years until 2032, with any further increases tied to cost-of-living adjustments. It's a big deal. The current minimum wage in NYC is $17, and at $30, it would be the highest city minimum wage in the country.

According to NYC's Comptroller, one quarter of the local workforce earns minimum wage, meaning an increase would impact over a million workers. The proposed bill also aims to phase out the tip credit for food service workers, while implementing robust reporting requirements. Any failure to maintain or produce wage records would create rebuttable presumptions of the employer, which must provide formal notice to employees about their rights on an annual basis.

Outlook: "The math ain't mathing," said Brooklyn City Council Member Sandy Nurse, who put forward the "30 for Our City" Act. "$17 per hour is not a livable wage. It is a crisis. This is not a dignified life." Others argue that such a sharp increase could deepen monopolies and pressure small businesses, which are already facing sky-high rent and tight profit margins. Additional fears center around higher prices due to increased labor costs, as well as an increase in the unemployment rate, especially among low-skilled workers.

data.title: Kyivstar Group jumps after posting double-digit revenue growth
data.description:
• Kyivstar Group (KYIV) announced FY25 revenue rose 25.9% Y/Y to $1.16B; 4Q25 revenue increased 28.4% Y/Y to $321M.
  • EBITDA for FY25 climbed 25.8% Y/Y to $648M, with a margin of 56.0%.
  • Adjusted net profit reached $289M, with EPS at $1.32, beating the consensus estimate of $1.19 by $0.13.
  • Unadjusted net profit for 4Q25 was $90M.
  • Digital revenue jumped 4.7x Y/Y to $124M, accounting for 10.7% of total revenue, reflecting strong growth in the company’s digital ecosystem.
  • Outlook: Kyivstar Group (KYIV) expects 2026 USD revenue growth of 8%–11% and EBITDA growth of 5%–8%, assuming UAH/USD at 44.5; capex intensity is expected at 23%–26% of revenue.
  • CEO Oleksandr Komarov said, “We enter 2026 with strong momentum, supported by accelerating revenue growth. In 4Q25, digital revenue expanded more than sixfold, reaching nearly 16% of total revenue. Our 30% Y/Y revenue growth in hryvnia reflects successful execution of our digital expansion roadmap and deeper group-wide synergies, with meaningful contributions across business verticals.”
  • Kyivstar Group (KYIV) shares rose around 4.9% to $10.70 in Thursday premarket hours.
